title: Linaria × grandiflora
taxonomy: Linaria × grandiflora is a hybrid species, and its classification is that of a toadflax. As a hybrid, it is not classified into a single species category but rather as a cross between two parent species. The classification of Linaria × grandiflora is generally agreed upon as belonging to the family Plantaginaceae. The exact parent species of Linaria × grandiflora may vary depending on the specific cultivar. However, its classification remains a member of the Linaria genus.
uses: Linaria × grandiflora is known for its potential medicinal uses, although more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness. The plant's flowers and leaves have been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various ailments. Some studies suggest that Linaria × grandiflora may possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Additionally, it has been used to treat respiratory issues such as bronchitis and coughs. Further research is necessary to fully understand the plant's medicinal benefits and potential uses.

constituents: Linaria × grandiflora contains a variety of active constituents, including flavonoids and iridoid glycosides. The flavonoids present in this plant, such as kaempferol and quercetin, are known for their antioxidant properties. Iridoid glycosides, such as catalpol and ajugol, have been found to possess anti-inflammatory and vasodilatory effects. preparations: Linaria × grandiflora is not a well-documented plant in herbal preparations, but it's possible that its flowers or leaves might be used in traditional medicine, similar to other species in the Linaria genus. intro: Linaria × grandiflora, also known as Toadflax or Butter and Eggs, is a hybrid plant species in the family Plantaginaceae, characterized by its bright yellow, trumpet-shaped flowers. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including respiratory problems, such as bronchitis and asthma, due to its expectorant and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents of Linaria × grandiflora include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Herbal preparations made from the plant include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to soothe coughs and alleviate respiratory discomfort.
